case_name: RE. BLANCHARD AND DISCIPLINARY BOARD OF MILLHAVEN INSTITUTION AND HARDTMAN
court: Federal Court, Trial Division Addy J.
body_html:
March 15, 1982

Prisons -- Inmates' rights -- Disciplinary hearings -- Inmate charged with acts calculated to prejudice discipline or good order of institution -- Penitentiary disciplinary board not required to act on judicial or quasi-judicial basis but under over-all duty to act fairly -- Nature of hearing required to be conducted -- Nature of duty on board -- Penitentiary Service Regulations, C.R.C. 1978, c. 1251, s. 39.

A hearing conducted by a penitentiary disciplinary board for an alleged infraction of Penitentiary Service Regulations, C.R.C. 1978, c. 1251, such as charges of doing acts calculated to prejudice the discipline or good order of the institution contrary to s. 39 Of the Penitentiary Service Regulations, is an administrative proceeding and is neither judicial nor quasi-judicial in character. Nevertheless the board is under a duty to act fairly and this requires that the inquiry conducted by the board be carried on in a fair manner with due regard to natural justice. Thus this would require that the inmate be aware of what the allegations are, of the evidence and the nature of the evidence against him and that he be afforded a reasonable opportunity to respond to the evidence and to give his version of the matter. Where evidence is being given orally therefore, the inmate should be present and also be afforded the opportunity of cross-examining or questioning any witnesses unless there are exceptional circumstances which would render such a hearing practically impossible or very difficult to conduct. There is no duty, however, on the board to explore every conceivable defence or to suggest possible defences to an inmate although there is a duty to conduct a full and fair inquiry which may lead to the obligation of asking questions of the inmate or of any witness the answers to which might prove exculpatory. There is no right to counsel, and whether counsel representing an inmate is to be allowed to be present is a matter for the discretion of the chairman conducting the inquiry. Finally, the inmate must be mentally and physically capable of understanding the proceedings and the nature and details of the accusations, of taking cognizance of any oral or written evidence presented, of questioning witnesses and of presenting his version of the matter. Since the hearing is an inquisitorial proceeding and not an adversary proceeding, and since hearsay evidence is admissible, it is not up to the courts to review the evidence as it might review the evidence of a judicial tribunal; the courts are merely to consider whether there has in fact been a breach of the general duty to act fairly, and unless there has been a patent disregard of the evidence indicating bad faith on the part of the board or a breach of the general duty to act fairly, the courts will not intervene.

APPLICATION by an inmate pursuant to s. 18 of the Federal Court Act, R.S.C. 1970, c. 10 (2nd Supp.), for a writ of certiorari to quash his conviction on two counts contrary to s. 39 of the Penitentiary Service Regulations (Can.).

A. J. MacPhail, for applicant.

L. S. Holland, for respondents.

ADDY J.:-- Upon application of the applicant pursuant to s. 18 of the Federal Court Act, R.S.C. 1970, c. 10 (2nd Supp.), for a writ of certiorari or relief in the nature thereof, quashing the decisions of the respondent Douglas L. Hardtman made on or about February 10, 1982, convicting the applicant of two counts of doing "an act calculated to prejudice the discipline or good order of the Institution" contrary to s. 39(k) of the Penitentiary Service Regulations, C.R.C. 1978, c. 1251, proclaimed pursuant to the Penitentiary Act, R.S.C. 1970, c. P-6; one count of damaging government property contrary so s. 39(3) of the Penitentiary Service Regulations proclaimed pursuant to the Penitentiary Act; one count of being "... indecent, disrespectful or threatening in his actions, language or writing toward any other person" contrary to s. 39(g) of the Penitentiary Service Regulations proclaimed pursuant to the Penitentiary Act; one count of having "contraband in his possession" contrary to s. 39(i) of the Penitentiary Service Regulations proclaimed pursuant to the Penitentiary Act, one count of assault contrary to s. 39(b) of the Penitentiary Service Regulations proclaimed pursuant to the Penitentiary Act, and quashing the decisions of the respondent Douglas L. Hardtman made on or about February 17, 1982, convicting the applicant of two counts of "doing an act calculated to prejudice the discipline or good order of the Institution" contrary to s. 39(k) of the Penitentiary Service Regulations proclaimed pursuant to the Penitentiary Act.

REASONS

A hearing conducted by a penitentiary disciplinary board for an alleged infraction of Penitentiary Service Regulations is an administrative proceeding and is neither judicial nor quasi-judicial in character.

Except to the extent that there are statutory provisions or regulations having the force of law to the contrary, there is no requirement to conform to any particular procedure or to abide by the rules of evidence generally applicable to judicial or quasi-judicial tribunals or adversary proceedings.

There is, however, an over-all duty to act fairly in administrative matters and, when applied to an administrative hearing or inquiry, the duty to act fairly translates into one of ensuring that the inquiry is carried out in a fair manner and with due regard to natural justice. This duty to act fairly where the conduct of a person who might be subject to some penalty is being examined, requires that the person be aware of what the allegations are, of the evidence and the nature of the evidence against him and be afforded a reasonable opportunity to respond to the evidence and to give his version of the matter. In order to achieve this, wherever evidence is being given orally, the prisoner should be present and also be afforded the opportunity of cross-examining or questioning any witness, unless there are exceptional circumstances which would render such a hearing practically impossible or very difficult to conduct, such as deliberately obstructive conduct on the part of the party concerned.

There is no general right to have the proceedings transcribed verbatim but, where such a transcription exists, as in the present case, it may be used to enable the reviewing court to come to its conclusions on the merits of the application.

Although the hearing is not to be conducted as an adversary proceeding but as an inquisitorial one, there is no duty on the person responsible for conducting the hearing to explore every conceivable defence or to suggest possible defences to the prisoner, although there is a duty to conduct a full and fair inquiry which, of course, might lead to the obligation of asking questions of the prisoner or of any witness, the answers to which might prove exculpatory in so far as the prisoner is concerned. He must, in other words, examine both sides of the question.

There is no right to counsel; whether counsel representing the prisoner is to be allowed to be present is a matter for the discretion of the chairman conducting the inquiry. Occasions might possibly arise where matters are so complicated from a legal standpoint that the duty to act fairly might require the presence of counsel, but I cannot at the moment envisage such a situation, especially where the person conducting the inquiry is a legally qualified barrister and solicitor, as in the present case. Furthermore, the questions arising in these disciplinary proceedings are, generally, of a factual nature.

The prisoner must be mentally and physically capable of understanding the proceedings and the nature and details of the accusations, of taking cognizance of any oral or written evidence presented, of questioning witnesses and of presenting his version of the matter. Where there is any doubt as to the prisoner's capability to so take part in the proceedings, then, in order to act fairly, the chairman must first satisfy himself on that issue before proceeding with the hearing.

After having examined the affidavits and exhibits produced on this present application, including the transcript of the oral evidence (albeit a very incomplete one), hearing the arguments of counsel and considering the jurisprudence referred to, I cannot, in the light of the above principles, conclude that the chairman acted unfairly in any way.

He carefully took into consideration the prisoner's capacity to defend himself and concluded that he was so capable. There is no evidence that the prisoner was mentally incapable of taking part. On the contrary, he addressed some very pertinent questions to the witnesses and made statements which were quite relevant to the issues.

The chairman considered the request for representation by counsel and exercised his discretion to refuse counsel in the light of most recent jurisprudence on that subject. Unless the exercise of any such discretion is patently unfair, this court should not interfere.

ORDER

Application dismissed with costs.

Application dismissed.
